heuristics.rule: Report address changes to your DSO within 15 days (F-1) or 10 days (J-1) — no exceptions, no delays
heuristics.condition: When you move to a new apartment, dorm, or residence at any point during your program
heuristics.action: Send the address update email to your DSO the same day you move or within the first week at the latest; do not wait until it feels convenient
heuristics.because: Failure to report is a recorded SEVIS violation that surfaces during OPT, H-1B, and green card processing — causing RFEs, delays, and potential denial even years after the move

heuristics.rule: Never work a single day before your EAD card is physically in your hands
heuristics.condition: When you are waiting for an Employment Authorization Document and your start date is approaching
heuristics.action: Do not begin any compensated work — not one hour — until the physical EAD card is received, regardless of what your employer says about approval likelihood
heuristics.because: One unauthorized workday creates a permanent SEVIS record that can block H-1B approval and green card processing; the rationalization 'the EAD is almost here' does not constitute authorization

heuristics.rule: File for F-1 reinstatement within 5 months of the status violation — every day past that window dramatically reduces your chances
heuristics.condition: When you discover you have fallen out of F-1 status for any reason
heuristics.action: Immediately consult your DSO and an immigration attorney; begin assembling reinstatement paperwork without waiting to see if the situation resolves itself
heuristics.because: The 5-month window is a hard legal deadline; past it, USCIS reinstatement is unavailable, and unlawful presence continues accumulating toward the 180-day and 365-day re-entry bars

heuristics.rule: If you are on J-1, maintain health insurance continuously — treat a lapse as a five-alarm emergency, not an administrative inconvenience
heuristics.condition: When your J-1 health insurance coverage expires, lapses, or is about to lapse for any reason
heuristics.action: Reinstate or purchase qualifying coverage immediately and notify your DSO; document the lapse duration and cause
heuristics.because: Willful failure to maintain J-1 health insurance is uniquely catastrophic: it terminates J-1 status AND bars reinstatement permanently — there is no recovery path

heuristics.rule: Treat pre-graduation status violations as active threats even after you receive your degree
heuristics.condition: When you are applying for OPT, H-1B, or any post-completion benefit after graduating
heuristics.action: Before filing any post-graduation immigration benefit, review your full SEVIS compliance history with a DSO or attorney and resolve any outstanding issues proactively
heuristics.because: USCIS cross-references SEVIS records against immigration applications; violations that occurred before graduation surface during OPT and H-1B processing and cause denials or RFEs

heuristics.rule: If on J-1, check whether you are subject to the two-year home residency requirement before making any H-1B or immigrant visa plans
heuristics.condition: When a J-1 student begins planning their post-graduation immigration pathway
heuristics.action: Request a two-year rule determination from your DSO or review your DS-2019 and funding source; if subject, pursue waiver options early
heuristics.because: The two-year home residency requirement blocks H-1B and immigrant visa applications; students who ignore it build career plans on a foundation that collapses at visa application time

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Willful violations permanently disqualify a student from reinstatement and are treated far more severely in future immigration proceedings than technical or unknowing violations
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Determine whether the student had knowledge of the rule at the time of violation; documented DSO communications, enrollment records, and prior warnings are evidence of knowledge

decision_factors.why_it_matters: At 180 days, a 3-year re-entry bar triggers; at 365 days, a 10-year bar triggers — these bars apply even if the student was unaware of the violation
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Calculate from the date the status violation occurred (not the date of discovery); cross-reference I-94 records, SEVIS history, and enrollment records

decision_factors.factor: Type of visa status at time of violation (F-1 vs. J-1)
decision_factors.why_it_matters: J-1 violations are categorically more severe: J-1 termination requires immediate departure with no grace period, and certain J-1 violations (health insurance) have no reinstatement path
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Identify the student's exact visa classification; do not assume F-1 and J-1 rules are interchangeable

decision_factors.factor: Whether the violation involved unauthorized employment
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Unauthorized employment is the most severe individual violation; it typically ends F-1 status with no reinstatement option and creates a permanent SEVIS record
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Review any compensated activity, including consulting, freelance, stipends, or informal arrangements, against the student's authorized work status at the time

decision_factors.why_it_matters: The 5-month reinstatement window is a hard cutoff; students who discover violations late lose their reinstatement eligibility before they can act
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Pinpoint the exact date the violation began (e.g., first day of reduced course load, date of move without reporting) and calculate how many days have elapsed

decision_factors.why_it_matters: DHS can revoke a university's F-1 certification and State Department can revoke J-1 program designation, affecting all international students at that institution — not just the individual violator
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Consider whether the student's violation involves systemic DSO failures (e.g., improper I-20 issuance, inadequate financial verification) that expose the institution

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Criminal conviction is an automatic J-1 termination trigger; any criminal record also blocks F-1 reinstatement eligibility
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Review whether any arrests, charges, or institutional disciplinary actions are on record; even pending charges can affect immigration status

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Skipping health insurance as a J-1 student to save money
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: A lapse classified as willful permanently terminates J-1 status with zero reinstatement path — the student must depart immediately and cannot recover their status through any domestic process
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Healthy J-1 students who perceive health insurance as unnecessary expense and let coverage lapse between semesters or during breaks

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Starting work one day before EAD physically arrives
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: That single day becomes a permanent unauthorized employment record in SEVIS that can block H-1B approvals and green card petitions years in the future, long after the student has forgotten the incident
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students in time-pressured job start situations where employers pressure early starts and students assume imminent EAD approval equals authorization

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Waiting to see if a status violation resolves itself before acting
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Each day of inaction is a day of accumulating unlawful presence; the student may cross the 180-day or 365-day bar before discovering the violation, triggering multi-year re-entry bars that apply even if the student later departs voluntarily
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who are unaware of the violation at the time it occurs and only discover it during a future immigration filing

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Assuming graduation erases prior compliance issues
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: SEVIS records are permanent; violations from sophomore year surface during H-1B or green card processing 3–5 years later, creating RFEs, delays, and potential denial at a high-stakes career moment
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who had minor violations earlier in their program and assumed time or graduation would erase the record

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Not disclosing a known status violation on immigration forms
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: USCIS compares student self-reporting against SEVIS records; discrepancies constitute misrepresentation, which is a separate and more severe immigration violation that can result in permanent inadmissibility
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who know about a past violation and hope to conceal it on H-1B or green card applications

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Accepting a J-1 visa instead of F-1 for the added institutional support
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: J-1 comes with significantly harsher violation consequences, a potential two-year home residency requirement, and mandatory health insurance; students who choose J-1 for its benefits often do not understand the asymmetric downside risk
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students offered J-1 sponsorship by their university or a program without fully understanding J-1-specific obligations versus F-1

failure_patterns.pattern: Failing to report address changes within the required window
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students treat address reporting as administrative paperwork rather than a legal obligation; the consequence feels distant and the action feels low-priority during a move
failure_patterns.consequence: SEVIS records a compliance failure that surfaces during H-1B or OPT processing as an RFE or denial; even a single unreported move can delay H-1B approval by 3–4 months and require attorney intervention
warning_signs: Student moved in the past year but cannot recall notifying DSO
DSO address on file does not match current residence
Student unaware of the 15-day (F-1) or 10-day (J-1) reporting deadline

failure_patterns.pattern: Working one day before EAD card arrives
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students rationalize that approved = authorized; the physical card feels like a formality when approval notice is already received
failure_patterns.consequence: Permanent unauthorized employment record in SEVIS; F-1 status termination; reinstatement typically unavailable for unauthorized employment; long-term H-1B and green card complications
warning_signs: Employer sets start date before student's EAD card has arrived
Student says 'my EAD was approved, I'm just waiting for the card'
Student unsure whether approval notice vs. physical card constitutes work authorization

failure_patterns.pattern: J-1 student letting health insurance lapse
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students who are young and healthy perceive health insurance as an unnecessary cost; insurance renewals happen on administrative cycles students don't track
failure_patterns.consequence: Willful health insurance failure terminates J-1 status permanently with no reinstatement path; student must depart the US immediately
warning_signs: J-1 student shopping for cheaper non-qualifying coverage
Student unaware that health insurance is federally mandated for J-1 (not just recommended)
Insurance expired between academic terms without renewal

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming pre-graduation violations won't affect post-graduation immigration benefits
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students mentally compartmentalize their student years from their professional immigration pathway; graduation feels like a reset
failure_patterns.consequence: OPT denial, H-1B RFE or denial, green card complications triggered by violations from years earlier that were never resolved
warning_signs: Student graduated but never addressed a known compliance gap from earlier in their program
Student cannot recall full enrollment and address history during their degree
Student has never reviewed their SEVIS record

failure_patterns.pattern: Concealing or underreporting status violations on immigration forms
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students fear that disclosure will harm their application and hope the record won't be found; they underestimate USCIS's access to SEVIS history
failure_patterns.consequence: Misrepresentation is a separate, more severe violation than the original status issue; can result in permanent inadmissibility, which is far worse than the original violation
warning_signs: Student says 'I'll just leave that blank' when asked about immigration history
Student unsure whether a past event counts as a violation and decides not to mention it
Discrepancy between student's narrative and what DSO records show

failure_patterns.pattern: Delaying reinstatement action after discovering an F-1 status violation
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students hope the situation will resolve, fear the complexity of reinstatement, or are unaware of the hard 5-month deadline
failure_patterns.consequence: Every day of delay adds unlawful presence; past 5 months, USCIS reinstatement is unavailable; past 180 days, a 3-year re-entry bar activates; past 365 days, a 10-year bar activates
warning_signs: Student discovered a potential violation but has not yet contacted DSO or attorney
Student is 'waiting to see what happens' with a compliance gap
More than 2 months have passed since the violation began without action

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you moved to a new address at any point during your program without notifying your DSO within 15 days (F-1) or 10 days (J-1)?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether a SEVIS address reporting violation exists that could surface as an RFE or denial during OPT, H-1B, or green card processing
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: When exactly did you move, and when did you notify your DSO? Calculate the gap and assess whether it exceeds the reporting window; if so, consult a DSO or attorney immediately
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Verify by cross-referencing DSO records with actual move dates — students often underestimate or misremember the reporting timeline

diagnostic_questions.question: Did you perform any compensated work — formal or informal — before your EAD card was physically in your possession?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether an unauthorized employment violation exists in the student's SEVIS record, which is one of the most severe and least recoverable F-1 violations
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Consult an immigration attorney immediately; determine whether reinstatement is still possible and assess the impact on pending or future immigration filings
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Confirm the student understands that approval notice ≠ work authorization; the physical EAD card must be received before any work begins

diagnostic_questions.question: Are you on J-1 status, and is your health insurance currently active and continuously maintained?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is at risk of the single most unrecoverable J-1 violation; health insurance is federally mandated for J-1, and willful lapses permanently terminate status
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm the coverage meets J-1 federal requirements (minimum coverage limits, repatriation, medical evacuation); not all health insurance qualifies
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Assess whether the lapse is willful; if so, the student must consult an immigration attorney immediately as reinstatement is not available for willful J-1 health insurance failures

diagnostic_questions.question: If you are on J-1, have you confirmed whether you are subject to the two-year home residency requirement?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student's planned H-1B or immigrant visa pathway is legally viable without first obtaining a waiver or completing home country residency
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Begin exploring waiver options (interested government agency, no-objection statement, hardship) well before the H-1B filing deadline
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Review DS-2019 funding source and J-1 category with DSO; government-funded or skills-list J-1 holders are typically subject to the requirement

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you ever been out of status — even briefly — at any point during your studies, and did you take formal steps to resolve it at the time?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether unresolved historical violations are sitting in the SEVIS record that will surface during future immigration benefit applications
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Document the exact violation, duration, and resolution steps; if no formal resolution occurred, consult an attorney before filing any new immigration benefit
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Walk through each potential violation category systematically (enrollment gaps, address moves, work authorization) rather than relying on the student's general recollection

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you maintained full-time enrollment every semester, including any semester you dropped below full-time without a formal DSO-approved reduced course load authorization?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether an enrollment-based status violation exists; failure to maintain full-time enrollment is one of the primary F-1 and J-1 status termination triggers
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Determine whether the reduced enrollment was formally authorized; check DSO records for a reduced course load approval on file
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Identify the semester(s) in question, calculate the unlawful presence that may have accumulated, and assess reinstatement eligibility if within the 5-month window

industry_realities.common_belief: If I graduate without incident, any past compliance issues are behind me and won't affect my career immigration process
industry_realities.actual_reality: SEVIS records are permanent and time-stamped; USCIS routinely cross-references SEVIS history against H-1B, OPT, and green card applications — violations from early in a student's program surface years later with full documentation
industry_realities.implication: Students must audit their SEVIS compliance history before filing any post-graduation immigration benefit, not after receiving an RFE

industry_realities.common_belief: Status violations only matter if someone catches them at the time they happen
industry_realities.actual_reality: Unlawful presence accumulates from the date of violation, not the date of discovery; a student can cross the 180-day bar and trigger a multi-year re-entry ban without anyone notifying them — the first notice often comes at a port of entry or during a visa application abroad
industry_realities.implication: Students must act on potential violations immediately upon discovery, not wait to see if they become a problem

industry_realities.common_belief: An EAD approval notice means I am authorized to work
industry_realities.actual_reality: Work authorization for F-1 OPT requires the physical EAD card; the approval notice does not constitute authorization — one day of work before the card arrives is a permanent SEVIS unauthorized employment record
industry_realities.implication: Students must structure their employment start date around EAD card receipt, not approval notice date; employers should be educated on this distinction

industry_realities.common_belief: J-1 and F-1 are essentially similar student visas with the same rules
industry_realities.actual_reality: J-1 violations are categorically more severe: J-1 termination requires immediate departure with no grace period; certain J-1 violations (health insurance) have no reinstatement path; and J-1 holders may face a two-year home residency requirement that F-1 holders do not
industry_realities.implication: Students choosing between F-1 and J-1 — or advising others — must treat them as fundamentally different compliance regimes, not equivalent alternatives

industry_realities.common_belief: Address reporting is an administrative formality with no real enforcement consequence
industry_realities.actual_reality: Address change failures are among the most commonly triggered SEVIS violations precisely because students underestimate them; they consistently appear in H-1B RFEs and cause 3–4 month processing delays and attorney fees
industry_realities.implication: DSOs should proactively remind students of the 15-day (F-1) and 10-day (J-1) rules at every housing transition point; students should treat it like a legal filing deadline

industry_realities.common_belief: If I disclose a past violation on an immigration form, it will automatically disqualify my application
industry_realities.actual_reality: Concealing a known violation and having it discovered through SEVIS cross-referencing constitutes misrepresentation — a separate and more severe violation that can result in permanent inadmissibility, far worse than the original violation
industry_realities.implication: Students with known compliance gaps should disclose proactively with attorney-prepared explanatory documentation rather than attempt concealment

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Understanding of when unlawful presence begins
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Student believes unlawful presence starts when USCIS sends a notice or when someone 'catches' the violation
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Student knows unlawful presence begins accumulating on the date the violation occurs, regardless of whether anyone has identified or notified them — and proactively checks their SEVIS record after any compliance gap
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Students who wait for notification can cross the 180-day and 365-day bars before taking action, triggering 3- and 10-year re-entry bans that cannot be easily reversed

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Treating F-1 and J-1 as equivalent
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Student or advisor treats both visas as 'student visas' with essentially the same rules, consequences, and recovery options
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Student distinguishes them as fundamentally different compliance regimes: J-1 has harsher immediate consequences, mandatory health insurance with no reinstatement for willful failure, no grace period upon termination, and a potential two-year home residency requirement
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: A student who applies F-1 logic to a J-1 situation — such as assuming they have time to fix a health insurance lapse — can permanently lose their status with no recovery path

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Student never reviews their SEVIS record and assumes that if nothing went wrong that they know about, their record is clean
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Student reviews SEVIS history with DSO before filing OPT, H-1B, or any major immigration benefit; identifies and addresses any reportable gaps proactively with documentation
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Unresolved historical violations discovered during H-1B processing cause RFEs, delays, and denials at the worst possible career moment — when the student is transitioning from OPT and has limited time to respond

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Response to discovering a status violation
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Student waits to see if anything happens, assumes it might not matter, or delays acting because the reinstatement process seems complex
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Student immediately contacts DSO and an immigration attorney, calculates the exact number of days since the violation began, and initiates reinstatement paperwork before the 5-month window closes
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The 5-month reinstatement window is a hard cutoff; delay converts a recoverable situation into a permanent status termination requiring departure

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Understanding of what constitutes work authorization
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Student believes OPT approval notice or employer offer letter is sufficient to begin work; treats physical EAD card as a formality
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Student knows that physical EAD card receipt is the legal start of work authorization; structures start date negotiations around card receipt, not approval date, and educates HR accordingly
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Beginning work even one day early creates a permanent unauthorized employment record in SEVIS that cannot be expunged and can block future H-1B and green card filings

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Student treats address reporting, enrollment certification, and insurance maintenance as low-priority administrative tasks that can be done whenever convenient
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Student treats each reporting requirement as a hard legal deadline with a specific calendar trigger: move date + 15 days (F-1), move date + 10 days (J-1), insurance renewal dates tracked proactively
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: These 'administrative' failures are the most common source of SEVIS violations and routinely appear in H-1B RFEs; the student who treats them as legal deadlines avoids the single most avoidable category of immigration problems
